Title: AtPHT4;4 is a chloroplast-localized ascorbate transporter in Arabidopsis

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Landsberg ecotype; Columbia wild type used for expression analysis
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antioxidant and coenzyme for metabolic reactions; required in chloroplasts for photoprotection and for violaxanthin de-epoxidase-dependent heat dissipation in the xanthophyll cycle
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: chloroplasts, especially chloroplast stroma/envelope-associated pool in leaves
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Phosphate transporter 4 family (PHT4); plant SLC17 transporter family membrane transporter
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly expressed in leaves, especially palisade tissue chloroplasts; higher in leaves than roots; induced by light
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed in leaves; induced by light
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes chloroplast ascorbate accumulation; loss of function decreases reduced ascorbate in leaves and chloroplast fraction, impairs xanthophyll-cycle-dependent nonphotochemical quenching, lowers zeaxanthin/antheraxanthin under high light, and reduces photoprotection under strong light
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Purified protein reconstitution into proteoliposomes with transport assay; antibody-based subcellular localization; qPCR; knockout mutant analysis in two Ds insertion lines; leaf/chloroplast ascorbate quantification; chlorophyll fluorescence NPQ assays; HPLC pigment profiling
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtPHT4;4 was the only tested Arabidopsis PHT4 family member showing strong membrane potential-driven ascorbate uptake in proteoliposomes. 2. Transport was Cl--dependent and specific for reduced L-ascorbate, supporting AtPHT4;4 as an ascorbate transporter rather than a general phosphate transporter. 3. AtPHT4;4 localizes to the chloroplast envelope membrane and is highly expressed in light-exposed leaves. 4. atpht4;4 knockout lines showed ~35% lower reduced ascorbate in leaves under high light and reduced chloroplast ascorbate. 5. Mutants displayed decreased NPQ and reduced zeaxanthin/antheraxanthin formation, linking AtPHT4;4-mediated ascorbate transport to xanthophyll-cycle photoprotection.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Gene expression profiling cited from prior studies; no accession reported for this paper; qPCR used in this study
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Light exposure; high-light stress (230 or 540 μmol photons m^-2 s^-1) after low-light growth; dark adaptation
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No obvious visible growth or morphology penalty reported in knockout mutants under tested conditions, but mutants showed compromised heat dissipation and likely reduced tolerance to strong light stress
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target for engineering photoinhibition-tolerant plants by enhancing chloroplast ascorbate import and xanthophyll-cycle capacity; transporter engineering may complement ascorbate biosynthesis traits
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Also retains Na+/Pi cotransport activity in vitro, interpreted as an evolutionarily conserved function rather than the main physiological role in chloroplasts; authors infer transport from cytosol into chloroplast. AtPHT4;1 is discussed only as a candidate thylakoid ascorbate transporter and is not functionally established here.
